_meta.description: Curated tonewood reference for luthiers. Two tiers: Tier 1 (primary + established) are proven guitar woods with full tonal and application data. Tier 2 (emerging picks) are species gaining traction with builders, included for exploration. All data derived from wood_species.json — this file is a curated view, not a separate source of truth.

tiers.tier_1: Proven tonewoods (primary + established) — full lutherie characterization
tiers.tier_2: Emerging picks — real builder interest, lutherie data may be partial

machining_notes.notes: Very easy to machine. Excellent for guitar bodies.
Open grain can tear. Use downcut or compression bits for edges.
Ring-porous. Tearout at pore boundaries. Downcut recommended.
Very soft, machines like butter. Watch for fuzzing on end grain.
Dense, uniform grain. Good for steam bending sides.
Oily wood with dramatic figure. Machines well with sharp tooling.

bubinga.name: Bubinga
bubinga.tone_character: warm, rich bass, articulate mids â€” prized for bass guitars

machining_notes.notes: Hard but machines cleanly. Figured pieces tear â€” use climb cuts.

cedar_spanish.name: Spanish Cedar
cedar_spanish.tone_character: warm â€” traditional classical guitar neck wood
cedar_spanish.sustainability: CITES Appendix III â€” plantation-grown available

machining_notes.notes: Aromatic, easy to machine. Can be fuzzy on end grain. Traditional classical guitar neck.
Aromatic dust can be irritating. Very soft â€” light clamping pressure.

cherry_black.name: Black Cherry
cherry_black.tone_character: warm, sweet mids, similar to mahogany

machining_notes.notes: Burns easily with dull tools. Beautiful darkening patina.

cocobolo.name: Cocobolo
cocobolo.tone_character: warm, rich, complex â€” among finest rosewoods

machining_notes.notes: Severe sensitizer for many people. Full dust extraction required. Oils lubricate cut.

cypress_bald.name: Bald Cypress
cypress_bald.tone_character: dry, percussive, bright â€” quintessential flamenco tonewood

machining_notes.notes: Naturally rot-resistant. Easy to machine. Light clamping pressure.
Resinous â€” gums up tooling. Clean cutters frequently.
Extremely hard. Use sharp carbide, light cuts. Dust is hazardous.
Hardest wood in registry. Extreme tool wear. Carbide only.
Dense but machines well. Similar working properties to cocobolo minus the allergen risk.
Extreme tool wear â€” carbide only. Lapachol in dust is irritating. Pre-drill everything.
Interlocked grain can tear. Contains calcium carbonate deposits â€” dulls tools.

jatoba.name: Jatoba (Brazilian Cherry)
jatoba.tone_character: bright, percussive, similar to rosewood but less complex
jatoba.sustainability: not threatened â€” common budget rosewood alternative

machining_notes.notes: Very hard â€” carbide tooling essential. Common budget fretboard wood.
Among hardest commercial woods. Carbide only. Pre-drill all fastener holes.

koa.name: Hawaiian Koa
koa.tone_character: warm, balanced, opens up with age like rosewood

machining_notes.notes: Interlocked grain tears on figured areas. Expensive â€” test on scrap.
Watch for tearout on interlocked grain areas
Very prone to burning. Keep sharp tools, adequate feed rate.

maple_soft.name: Soft Maple (Red Maple)
maple_soft.tone_character: warm maple tone, less bright than hard

machining_notes.notes: Yellow sulfur deposits in pores can stain. Blunts tools faster than expected.
More porous than white oak. Not ideal for finishing but popular for Telecaster builds.
Ring-porous grain tears easily. Compression bits recommended.

ovangkol.name: Ovangkol (Shedua)
ovangkol.tone_character: warm, complex â€” rosewood family character

machining_notes.notes: Interlocked grain tears easily. Related to bubinga.

pau_ferro.name: Pau Ferro (Bolivian Rosewood)
pau_ferro.sustainability: not CITES listed â€” popular rosewood substitute

machining_notes.notes: Oily wood. Clean tools frequently. Common Fender fretboard wood since 2017.

pine_eastern_white.name: Eastern White Pine
pine_eastern_white.tone_character: warm, resonant when aged â€” cigar box guitar tradition

machining_notes.notes: Resin pockets catch on cutter. Economical for test cuts.

poplar_yellow.name: Yellow Poplar (Tulipwood)
poplar_yellow.tone_character: neutral, similar to alder but less defined

machining_notes.notes: Excellent for templates, jigs, and practice bodies. Very economical.
Heat turns wood brown â€” keep feeds up to maintain purple color. Gummy at high temps.

redwood.name: Redwood (Old Growth)
redwood.sustainability: old growth restricted â€” salvage/reclaimed only

machining_notes.notes: Soft, stable, beautiful. Old growth salvage only.
Extremely rare and expensive. Test on scrap first. Dust is sensitizing.
Oily wood dulls tools fast. Dust is sensitizing - use extraction.

sapele.name: Sapele
sapele.tone_character: like mahogany but brighter, good midrange definition
sapele.sustainability: sustainable â€” most common mahogany alternative

machining_notes.notes: Interlocked grain tears on ribbon figure areas. Climb cut helps.

spruce_adirondack.name: Adirondack Spruce (Red Spruce)
spruce_adirondack.tone_character: powerful, headroom, loud â€” the pre-war dreadnought tonewood

machining_notes.notes: Stiffest North American spruce. Premium soundboard wood.

spruce_engelmann.name: Engelmann Spruce
spruce_engelmann.tone_character: warm, responsive, lower overtones than Sitka

machining_notes.notes: Softer than Sitka. Preferred for fingerstyle guitars. Very fragile â€” light cuts only.

spruce_european.name: European Spruce (Alpine/German)
spruce_european.tone_character: warm, complex, refined â€” the classical luthier's choice

machining_notes.notes: Very stiff for its weight. Quartersawn essential for soundboards.
Softwood tears easily across grain. Sharp tools essential.

spruce_white.name: White Spruce
spruce_white.tone_character: bright, even â€” common bracing wood

machining_notes.notes: Softwood, tears across grain. Sharp tools essential.

tasmanian_blackwood.name: Tasmanian Blackwood
tasmanian_blackwood.grain: straight to wavy, often highly figured
tasmanian_blackwood.tone_character: warm, balanced, opens up with age â€” koa alternative
tasmanian_blackwood.sustainability: sustainable, plantation-grown in Tasmania and Australia

machining_notes.notes: Machines beautifully. Premium koa alternative at lower cost.
Natural oils lubricate cuts but gum up tooling. Clean bits frequently.
Excellent machining properties. Similar to mahogany.

wenge.name: Wenge
wenge.tone_character: dark, emphasized bass, warm with controlled highs

machining_notes.notes: Extremely splintery â€” large open pores shatter. Dust is sensitizing. Sharp carbide only.
Interlocked grain requires care. Dramatic visual result worth the effort.

ziricote.name: Ziricote
ziricote.tone_character: warm, complex, rosewood-like with unique character

machining_notes.notes: Dramatic spider-web figure. Machines well. Related to bocote.

honey_mesquite.name: Honey Mesquite
honey_mesquite.tone_character: bright, dense, crystalline highs with strong sustain â€” similar to rosewood but more percussive
honey_mesquite.sustainability: abundant in Texas/Southwest US, invasive species â€” harvest is ecologically beneficial

machining_notes.notes: Extremely hard â€” dulls HSS quickly, use carbide. Interlocked grain tears easily on figured stock. Dust is irritating. Small blanks common due to tree form.
